reason: This passage from Exodus 3 depicts God expressing accountability for the welfare of the Hebrew people ('I have surely seen the affliction of my people which are in Egypt, and have heard their cry...I know their sorrows'). God states His intention to deliver them ('I am come down to deliver them'). Moses, who will become the Hebrew leader, has not yet accepted his role and has not demonstrated accountability for the people's welfare. The text shows God commissioning Moses to lead a rescue, but the accountability for the people's welfare demonstrated in this passage is God's, not the Hebrew leader's. Moses himself responds with uncertainty ('Who am I, that I should go unto Pharaoh?'), rather than demonstrating proactive accountability for his people.
